race.name: Otztaler Radmarathon
race.tagline: 227 km. 5,500m of climbing. Four Alpine passes. The Timmelsjoch at 2,474m. Europe's hardest single-day sportive.

vitals.registration: Lottery entry via oetztaler-radmarathon.com. Applications typically open January-March. Entry: EUR199 (+EUR5 lottery fee). Oversubscribed by 3-4x.
vitals.feed_zones: Feed stations at each pass summit and in valley sections. Well-stocked with Austrian and Italian food, gels, water, cola.
vitals.cutoff_time: Strict cutoffs at each pass. Must reach Timmelsjoch summit before closure. Total time limit approximately 13.5 hours.
route_options: Otztaler Radmarathon: 227 km (141 mi), 5,500 m gain — the full route (no shorter option)

climate.description: Late August in the high Austrian/Italian Alps delivers a full spectrum of conditions in a single day. The 6:30 AM start in Solden is cold (5-10C). Valley sections warm rapidly through the day (20-30C). The Timmelsjoch at 2,474m can approach freezing with severe wind chill, even in August. The route crosses the main Alpine ridge twice, exposing riders to weather changes from north to south slope.
challenges: Pre-dawn start at 6:30 AM in a mountain valley — temperatures as low as 5C
Timmelsjoch summit at 2,474m: wind chill can bring effective temperature below freezing
30+ km of climbing to the Timmelsjoch means prolonged cold exposure at altitude
Rapid weather changes when crossing the Alpine ridge — north and south slopes have different weather systems
Afternoon thunderstorms can hit the Timmelsjoch with no shelter available

terrain.surface: Well-maintained Austrian and Italian mountain roads. All tarmac throughout. The Timmelsjoch toll road is closed to motor traffic for the event — pristine conditions on an otherwise busy summer pass road.
features: Kuhtai (2,020m) — 18 km opening climb, steady 6-7% gradient through pine forest
Brenner Pass (1,370m) — historic Austria-Italy border crossing, lower altitude, fast approach
Jaufenpass (2,090m) — 15 km climb from the Italian side, tight switchbacks through South Tyrolean villages
Timmelsjoch (2,474m) — the crowning finale: 30+ km of climbing from Passeiertal, the last 10 km above treeline on exposed road. The highest point of the day and the defining challenge of the event.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 76.

course_description.character: An ultra-distance Alpine odyssey. The 227 km route is a loop from Solden that crosses the main Alpine ridge twice via four major passes. The first half — Kuhtai and the descent to Innsbruck, then south over the Brenner — builds gradually. The Jaufenpass introduces the Italian side with its tight switchbacks and South Tyrolean villages. Then comes the Timmelsjoch: 30+ km of climbing from the Passeiertal valley to 2,474m, the final 10 km above treeline in barren, exposed Alpine terrain. The Timmelsjoch is the race.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Timmelsjoch. 30+ km of climbing to 2,474m after 180+ km already in the legs. The final 10 km above treeline — exposed, cold, barren, with no shelter and no respite — is where 227 km of accumulated fatigue meets altitude and weather. This is the moment the entire ride has been building toward.

logistics.airport: Innsbruck (INN) — 45 minutes (closest, limited international flights). Munich (MUC) — 2.5 hours (best connections). Zurich (ZRH) — 3.5 hours.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Solden — the start/finish town. Hotels are ski-resort oriented and book out for the Otztaler weekend. Book immediately upon winning the lottery. Obergurgl (20 min further up the valley) and Langenfeld (15 min down-valley) offer alternatives. Some riders stay in Innsbruck and drive to Solden race morning (45 min — requires very early departure for the 6:30 AM start).
logistics.food: Austrian Tyrolean cuisine — Tiroler Grostl, Kasnocken, Wiener Schnitzel, strudel. Hearty, carb-friendly food. Feed stations offer a mix of Austrian and Italian provisions (the route crosses into Italy). Solden has a reasonable restaurant selection for a ski resort town.

history.origin_story: The first Otztaler Radmarathon in 1982 attracted 115 riders who set out from Innsbruck to tackle the Austrian-Italian Alps in a single day. The route was inspired by the historic trade roads that connected the Inntal with the Italian Passeiertal via the Timmelsjoch — one of the highest paved passes in the Eastern Alps. The Timmelsjoch toll road, normally busy with summer tourist traffic, agreed to close for the event. That closure — 30+ km of traffic-free Alpine road to 2,474m — became the event's defining feature and has remained the emotional and physical climax for over 40 years.
notable_moments: 1982: Inaugural edition — 115 riders from Innsbruck across four Alpine passes
Start relocated to Solden in the Otztal Valley
Growth forced lottery entry — now oversubscribed by 3-4x for 4,000 spots
The Timmelsjoch closure for the event became a cycling tradition — 30+ km of car-free Alpine road
Consistently ranked among Europe's hardest single-day cycling events
history.reputation: The definitive European ultra-distance sportive. In German-speaking cycling culture, the Otztaler is the benchmark — the event you train all year for and tell stories about for the rest of your life.

biased_opinion.summary: The Otztaler Radmarathon is not a ride for people who want a nice day out on their bike. It is 227 km and 5,500 meters of climbing across four Alpine passes, finishing with a 30+ km ascent to the Timmelsjoch at 2,474m. It crosses the Austrian-Italian border twice. It starts in pre-dawn cold and ends — if you're lucky — before the afternoon thunderstorms hit the summit. Since 1982, the Otztaler has been the benchmark for ultra-distance road cycling in Europe. It is not the most beautiful event (the Maratona wins that), not the most prestigious (L'Etape owns that), and not the most famous (La Marmotte predates it). It is simply, relentlessly, the hardest.
strengths: The Timmelsjoch: 30+ km of climbing to 2,474m as the day's finale — cycling's most dramatic crescendo
227 km ultra distance — a genuine single-day cycling marathon, not a sportive
Cross-border Alpine epic: Austria to Italy and back across the main Alpine ridge
weaknesses: Lottery entry with no guaranteed spot — massively oversubscribed
Remote Otztal Valley location — limited airport access and accommodation
The Timmelsjoch in bad weather is genuinely dangerous — snow, freezing temperatures, and 60 km/h winds at 2,474m
Limited international community — primarily German-speaking cycling culture
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The Otztaler is the hardest single-day road cycling event most people will ever attempt. The Timmelsjoch at 2,474m, after 180 km of climbing, is where you discover what you're actually made of. Not for the faint of heart, not for the undertrained, and not for anyone who thinks 'sportive' means 'casual.'

final_verdict.one_liner: Europe's hardest single-day sportive. 227 km. 5,500m climbing. The Timmelsjoch at 2,474m. Since 1982.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the ultimate single-day cycling challenge and can sustain 9-13 hours of Alpine riding. Yes if the Timmelsjoch at 2,474m after 180 km is your idea of a good time. Skip if 227 km exceeds your distance capability — there is no shorter route option. Skip if remote Austrian valley logistics are a dealbreaker.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want similar Alpine challenge with shorter distance: La Marmotte (177 km, 5,180m). If you want Dolomite scenery: Maratona dles Dolomites (138 km, 4,230m). If you want an even longer ultra: Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo (330 km).

videos.title: Ötztaler Radmarathon 2025: Rennbericht
videos.description:
Hier ist er - der Rennbericht des Ötztaler Radmarathon 2025. Macht es euch auf der Couch (oder auf der Rolle) bequem und taucht nochmal ein in die spannenden Details des ÖRM 2025.

Und nicht vergessen: Hört niemals auf zu träumen, wir sehen uns am 30.08.2026!

 
//

videos.title: Ötztaler Radmarathon 2024 - 15 Stunden Liveübertragung
videos.description:
Es ist soweit: Der Ötztaler Radmarathon LIVE!
Folge über 4000 Teilnehmer bei ihrem Traum, heute als Finisher über die Ziellinie zu rollen. Spannende Stories und Impressionen, Live-Schaltungen zu unseren Reportern auf der Strecke und viele interessante Gäste im ÖRM TV Studio in Sölden geben einen Blick hinter die Kulissen des 43. Ötztaler Radmarathons.
